stem: You are the obstetric registrar reviewing a 30-year-old woman on day 4 after an emergency lower segment caesarean section for failure to progress. She has new lower abdominal pain, foul-smelling lochia and a temperature of 38.6 °C since this morning. She is haemodynamically stable but tachycardic and uncomfortable. Her wound is intact with no discharge. There is no flank tenderness and the breasts are non-tender.

data_table.Vital signs: T 38.6 °C, HR 112, RR 20, SpO2 98% room air, BP 110/68 mmHg
data_table.Examination: Tender uterus on bimanual examination palpable just above the symphysis, foul-smelling lochia, clean caesarean wound, no flank tenderness, breasts soft, calves non-tender
data_table.Investigations: FBC WCC 16.8 x10^9/L, CRP 142 mg/L, UEC creatinine 64 micromol/L, lactate 1.8 mmol/L, urinalysis no nitrites, blood cultures sent, high vaginal swab sent, group B streptococcus status from booking visit unknown

lead_in: What is the most appropriate empirical antibiotic regimen?

options.text: Oral amoxicillin 500 mg three times daily and arrange community midwife review with follow-up at the postnatal clinic in 48 hours if symptoms have not settled or temperatures persist over the next observation period
options.rationale: Oral amoxicillin monotherapy does not cover anaerobes or gram-negative organisms commonly seen in postpartum endometritis and the patient warrants intravenous therapy. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Intravenous ampicillin 2 g 6 hourly, gentamicin 5 mg/kg daily and metronidazole 500 mg 12 hourly with admission for clinical reassessment at 48 hours and a switch to oral therapy 24 hours after clinical resolution
options.rationale: Australian guidance for postpartum endometritis is intravenous ampicillin, gentamicin and metronidazole, continued for 24 hours after clinical resolution before stepping down to oral cover. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Intravenous flucloxacillin 2 g 6 hourly alone as monotherapy because the most likely organism complicating post-caesarean infection is Staphylococcus aureus from the wound site rather than the endometrial cavity
options.rationale: Endometritis is typically polymicrobial; flucloxacillin alone misses anaerobes and gram-negatives. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Intravenous ceftriaxone 2 g once daily alone with admission for fluid resuscitation and reassessment at 24 hours and step-down to oral cefalexin if symptoms have improved by the time of consultant ward round
options.rationale: Ceftriaxone monotherapy lacks adequate anaerobic cover for endometritis; combination therapy or piperacillin-tazobactam is required. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Intravenous vancomycin 25 mg/kg loading and 15 mg/kg 12 hourly maintenance with intravenous metronidazole and arrange interventional radiology drainage if the bedside ultrasound shows retained products in the cavity
options.rationale: Vancomycin is not first-line for endometritis without proven MRSA; drainage is reserved for retained products or abscess on imaging. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

explanation.summary: Postpartum endometritis is a polymicrobial infection of the endometrial cavity that follows caesarean section more often than vaginal birth. Australian guidance recommends intravenous ampicillin, gentamicin and metronidazole as empirical first-line cover. Treatment is continued intravenously until 24 hours after clinical resolution and then stepped down to oral therapy. Retained products of conception or pelvic abscess require imaging and source control.
key_points: Empirical IV regimen: ampicillin plus gentamicin plus metronidazole
Treat IV until 24 hours afebrile and clinically well, then oral step-down
Most common after caesarean section, polymicrobial
Imaging if no response by 48-72 hours or features of retained products
Source control with evacuation or drainage when indicated
explanation.pearls: Failure to respond within 48-72 hours warrants pelvic ultrasound or CT for retained products, septic pelvic vein thrombophlebitis or abscess; ovarian vein thrombosis often presents with right-sided pain and fever unresponsive to antibiotics alone.

stem: You are the general practice registrar in a shared-care arrangement with a fertility clinic, reviewing a 28-year-old non-Aboriginal woman who has been trying to conceive for 14 months. She has a body mass index of 27 kg/m2, oligomenorrhoea since menarche, mild facial hirsutism and a polycystic ovarian morphology on pelvic ultrasound. Her semen analysis from her partner is normal and her hysterosalpingogram shows patent fallopian tubes. Pelvic examination is unremarkable.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.6 °C, HR 72, RR 14, SpO2 99% room air, BP 116/72 mmHg, BMI 27 kg/m2
data_table.Examination: Mild upper lip and chin hirsutism, no acanthosis nigricans, no abdominal masses, normal bimanual pelvic examination
data_table.Investigations: Day 21 progesterone 3 nmol/L (anovulatory cycle), AMH 52 pmol/L, fasting insulin slightly elevated, fasting glucose 5.1 mmol/L, TSH and prolactin within reference ranges, partner semen analysis normal, hysterosalpingogram tubes patent

lead_in: What is the most appropriate first-line pharmacological agent for ovulation induction?

options.text: Clomiphene citrate 50 mg daily on cycle days 2 to 6 with luteinising hormone monitoring every cycle, escalating to clomiphene 150 mg if ovulation does not occur on the lower dose after three completed treatment cycles
options.rationale: Letrozole has displaced clomiphene as first-line ovulation induction in PCOS because of higher ovulation and live birth rates per the PCOS international guideline. (Source: International Evidence-Based Guideline for PCOS)

options.text: Metformin 500 mg three times daily as monotherapy targeting weight reduction, ovulation restoration and improved insulin sensitivity over a 6 month trial before considering any second-line ovulation induction agent
options.rationale: Metformin monotherapy has limited efficacy for pregnancy in PCOS infertility; it may augment letrozole or clomiphene in selected women but is not first-line for ovulation induction. (Source: International Evidence-Based Guideline for PCOS)

options.text: Letrozole 2.5 mg daily on cycle days 2 to 6 with follicular tracking ultrasound and progesterone check, escalating by 2.5 mg per cycle to a maximum 7.5 mg if ovulation does not occur on the lower dose
options.rationale: Letrozole is first-line ovulation induction in PCOS, dosed 2.5 mg days 2 to 6 with cycle tracking and stepwise dose escalation to a maximum of 7.5 mg if anovulation persists. (Source: International Evidence-Based Guideline for PCOS)

options.text: Recombinant follicle-stimulating hormone subcutaneous injection daily from cycle day 3 with serial transvaginal follicular tracking from day 8 onwards under specialist supervision in a tertiary fertility clinic
options.rationale: Gonadotrophin injection is third-line after letrozole or clomiphene failure due to higher cost and ovarian hyperstimulation risk. (Source: International Evidence-Based Guideline for PCOS)

options.text: Laparoscopic ovarian drilling under general anaesthetic as a single procedure offering durable ovulation restoration with lower multiple pregnancy rates than oral ovulation induction agents in selected anovulatory women
options.rationale: Ovarian drilling is a second-line surgical option after pharmacological induction has failed and is not preferred initial therapy. (Source: International Evidence-Based Guideline for PCOS)

explanation.summary: Letrozole has replaced clomiphene as first-line ovulation induction in anovulatory PCOS infertility based on the 2018 international evidence-based guideline endorsed by RANZCOG. Letrozole is dosed 2.5 mg daily for 5 days from cycle day 2, with stepwise escalation by 2.5 mg per cycle to a maximum of 7.5 mg. Lifestyle optimisation should occur in parallel. Metformin is reserved as adjunct; gonadotrophins and ovarian drilling are later-line.
key_points: Letrozole 2.5 mg days 2-6 is first-line ovulation induction in PCOS
Stepwise dose escalation to 7.5 mg if anovulation persists
Clomiphene is now second-line after letrozole
Metformin is adjunctive, not primary ovulation induction
Gonadotrophins and ovarian drilling are third-line
explanation.pearls: Letrozole has lower multiple-pregnancy rates than clomiphene and a similar safety profile in women with PCOS; cycle tracking with mid-luteal progesterone (over 30 nmol/L) confirms ovulation and a positive response to therapy.

subtopic_detail: Severe pre-eclampsia magnesium sulfate seizure prophylaxis
stem: You are the obstetric registrar at a tertiary maternity hospital reviewing a 33-year-old primigravida at 35+5 weeks gestation referred from a peripheral hospital with severe pre-eclampsia. She has a frontal headache, visual scotomata and right upper quadrant pain. Her blood pressure has been 168/110 mmHg on repeated readings and her urinalysis shows 4+ protein. The fetal heart rate trace is reassuring. She has had a single 20 mg dose of oral nifedipine 30 minutes ago with persistent hypertension.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.8 °C, HR 92, RR 18, SpO2 98% room air, BP 168/110 mmHg
data_table.Examination: Awake and orientated, hyperreflexia 3+ patellar with 4-beat clonus, right upper quadrant tenderness, no peripheral oedema, no focal neurology, fundal height 36 cm
data_table.Investigations: Urinalysis 4+ protein, PCR 480 mg/mmol, platelets 98 x10^9/L, ALT 142 U/L, AST 168 U/L, creatinine 92 micromol/L, urate 0.42 mmol/L, FBC otherwise within reference ranges, CTG category 1

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate medication?

options.text: Oral methyldopa 250 mg three times daily titrated to target blood pressure of 130 to 140 over 80 to 90 mmHg over the next 24 to 48 hours of admission and obstetric specialist co-management on the antenatal ward
options.rationale: Methyldopa has slow onset and is used for chronic, not acute, control; severe hypertension with end-organ features needs intravenous or rapid-acting therapy. (Source: SOMANZ Hypertension in Pregnancy)

options.text: Intravenous hydralazine 5 mg bolus repeated every 20 minutes as needed and intravenous magnesium sulfate 6 g over 20 minutes loading dose, then commence intravenous magnesium sulfate 2 g per hour maintenance infusion
options.rationale: Intravenous magnesium sulfate loading dose is 4 g (not 6 g) over 15-20 minutes followed by 1 g/hour maintenance per SOMANZ. (Source: SOMANZ Hypertension in Pregnancy)

options.text: Intravenous labetalol 20 mg bolus repeated every 10 minutes to a maximum cumulative dose of 80 mg and intravenous magnesium sulfate 6 g loading then 3 g per hour maintenance with deep tendon reflex monitoring
options.rationale: Magnesium loading is 4 g and maintenance is 1 g per hour; the dosing in this option is incorrect. (Source: SOMANZ Hypertension in Pregnancy)

options.text: Intravenous labetalol 20 mg bolus titrated to target 140 to 150 over 90 to 100 mmHg and intravenous magnesium sulfate 4 g over 20 minutes then 1 g per hour maintenance with hourly deep tendon reflex monitoring
options.rationale: Australian SOMANZ guidance for severe pre-eclampsia: rapid blood pressure lowering with labetalol or hydralazine, magnesium sulfate 4 g over 20 minutes loading then 1 g per hour maintenance with reflex and respiratory rate monitoring. (Source: SOMANZ Hypertension in Pregnancy)

options.text: Intravenous glyceryl trinitrate infusion titrated to a target mean arterial pressure of 90 mmHg and oral diazepam 10 mg for seizure prophylaxis with continuous CTG and 4 hourly observations of blood pressure
options.rationale: Glyceryl trinitrate is reserved for pulmonary oedema; benzodiazepines are not standard seizure prophylaxis in pre-eclampsia (magnesium is superior). (Source: SOMANZ Hypertension in Pregnancy)

explanation.summary: Severe pre-eclampsia with neurological features warrants rapid blood pressure control with intravenous labetalol or hydralazine (or oral nifedipine) plus intravenous magnesium sulfate for seizure prophylaxis. Australian SOMANZ dosing for magnesium is 4 g over 20 minutes loading then 1 g per hour maintenance, with hourly monitoring of deep tendon reflexes, respiratory rate and urine output. The target blood pressure is 140 to 150 over 90 to 100 mmHg; faster reductions risk fetal compromise.
key_points: Severe pre-eclampsia: rapid BP control plus magnesium sulfate prophylaxis
Magnesium sulfate 4 g IV over 20 min loading then 1 g/hr maintenance
Target BP 140-150/90-100 mmHg, not normotension
Monitor reflexes, RR, urine output hourly; calcium gluconate antidote
Definitive treatment is delivery once stable
explanation.pearls: Magnesium toxicity progresses from loss of patellar reflexes to respiratory depression and cardiac arrest; the antidote is intravenous calcium gluconate 1 g over 10 minutes, given alongside cessation of the magnesium infusion.

stem: You are the obstetric registrar covering the delivery suite when you are called urgently to a vaginal birth in progress. The midwife reports that the fetal head has delivered, restituted to face the maternal left thigh, and then retracted against the perineum with no progress over the last 60 seconds despite routine downward axial traction. The mother is a primipara at 39+3 weeks with a recent fundal growth scan estimating the fetus at 4.2 kg. She is exhausted but cooperative.

data_table.Vital signs: Maternal HR 104, RR 22, SpO2 99% room air, BP 122/78 mmHg, fetal heart rate 90/min and falling
data_table.Examination: Cephalic delivery with turtle sign, head retracted against perineum, anterior shoulder lodged behind pubic symphysis, posterior shoulder not yet delivered, attempt at routine axial traction has failed
data_table.Investigations: Not applicable in the acute manoeuvre phase; documentation will follow delivery

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Apply firm sustained fundal pressure to assist anterior shoulder delivery while continuing downward axial traction on the fetal head and request another midwife to support the maternal abdomen during the manoeuvre
options.rationale: Fundal pressure is contraindicated in shoulder dystocia and increases the risk of brachial plexus injury and uterine rupture. (Source: RANZCOG: Shoulder Dystocia)

options.text: Apply forceps to the after-coming head and rotate the head 180 degrees to deliver the anterior shoulder while the assistant maintains continuous downward traction to dislodge the impacted shoulder from the symphysis
options.rationale: Forceps rotation is not part of the shoulder dystocia sequence and risks fetal injury; manoeuvres focus on widening the pelvic outlet and rotating the shoulder. (Source: RANZCOG: Shoulder Dystocia)

options.text: Proceed immediately to symphysiotomy under local anaesthetic to enlarge the pelvic outlet because the anterior shoulder is impacted behind the symphysis and time-critical fetal distress is rapidly developing
options.rationale: Symphysiotomy is a last-resort manoeuvre with significant maternal morbidity and is not the next step after a failed initial axial traction. (Source: RANZCOG: Shoulder Dystocia)

options.text: Cease all maternal effort, call the consultant obstetrician and request the operating theatre to be readied for an emergency Zavanelli manoeuvre with replacement of the head and caesarean section delivery
options.rationale: Zavanelli is the absolute last resort after the full HELPERR sequence has failed; the next manoeuvre is McRoberts plus suprapubic pressure. (Source: RANZCOG: Shoulder Dystocia)

options.text: Call for help, perform the McRoberts manoeuvre with hyperflexion of the maternal thighs onto the abdomen and apply suprapubic pressure aimed at displacing the anterior shoulder posteriorly while continuing axial traction
options.rationale: The HELPERR sequence starts with calling for help, McRoberts and suprapubic pressure; this combination resolves the majority of shoulder dystocias before internal manoeuvres are needed. (Source: RANZCOG: Shoulder Dystocia)

explanation.summary: Shoulder dystocia is recognised by the turtle sign and failure of routine axial traction. The HELPERR sequence begins with calling for help, evaluating for episiotomy, legs in McRoberts, suprapubic pressure, enter internal manoeuvres (Rubin and Woods screw), remove the posterior arm and roll. Fundal pressure is contraindicated. Zavanelli replacement and symphysiotomy are last-resort manoeuvres. Most resolve with McRoberts plus suprapubic pressure alone.
key_points: Diagnosis: turtle sign and failed axial traction
HELPERR: Help, Evaluate episiotomy, Legs (McRoberts), Pressure (suprapubic), Enter manoeuvres, Remove posterior arm, Roll
Fundal pressure is contraindicated
Most resolve with McRoberts plus suprapubic pressure
Zavanelli and symphysiotomy are last resort
explanation.pearls: Document time intervals between manoeuvres, the order applied and which shoulder was anterior; brachial plexus injury risk is highest with prolonged head-to-body delivery interval and inappropriately applied lateral traction.

stem:
You are the obstetric registrar in the delivery suite called urgently to a 28-year-old G2P1 woman who has just delivered a 4.0 kg baby vaginally after a prolonged second stage. Estimated blood loss is now 1200 mL and ongoing. The uterus is boggy and poorly contracted on palpation. There is no retained placenta and no obvious trauma to the genital tract on rapid inspection.

She has known chronic hypertension controlled on oral labetalol. Intravenous oxytocin 10 units has already been given by the midwife as part of active management.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.9 °C, HR 118, RR 22, SpO2 99% room air, BP 102/68 mmHg
data_table.Examination: Boggy poorly contracted uterus palpable at umbilicus, ongoing per-vaginal blood loss, no genital tract trauma identified, placenta delivered intact, IV access wide bore in both antecubital fossae
data_table.Investigations: FBC pre-delivery Hb 118 g/L, blood type O Rh-positive, group and hold sent, baseline coagulation profile within reference ranges, second large-bore cannula sited

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next pharmacological step?

options.text: Commence an oxytocin infusion 40 units in 1000 mL Hartmann's at 250 mL/hour, give rectal misoprostol 800 micrograms and intravenous tranexamic acid 1 g over 10 minutes alongside ongoing bimanual uterine massage
options.rationale: With ergometrine contraindicated in chronic hypertension, the next steps are an oxytocin infusion plus misoprostol plus TXA within 3 hours of PPH onset with continued mechanical measures. (Source: RANZCOG: PPH)

options.text: Commence an oxytocin infusion 40 units in 1000 mL Hartmann's at 250 mL/hour, give intramuscular carboprost (15-methyl PGF2 alpha) 250 micrograms and add intravenous tranexamic acid 1 g over 10 minutes
options.rationale: Carboprost should be avoided in asthmatic patients and is generally a third-line uterotonic; misoprostol or oxytocin infusion plus TXA come first in Australia. (Source: RANZCOG: PPH)

options.text: Commence an oxytocin infusion 40 units in 1000 mL Hartmann's at 250 mL/hour, give rectal misoprostol 600 micrograms and arrange a balloon tamponade device for insertion if bleeding continues
options.rationale: Tranexamic acid 1 g IV should be given within 3 hours of PPH onset; omitting TXA misses a major guideline-recommended step. (Source: RANZCOG: PPH)

options.text: Commence intravenous norepinephrine titrated to mean arterial pressure 65 mmHg and give intravenous tranexamic acid 1 g over 10 minutes while preparing for emergency examination under anaesthesia in theatre
options.rationale: Vasopressors are not first-line in early PPH where the priority is uterine tone, volume resuscitation and pharmacological haemostasis. (Source: RANZCOG: PPH)

options.text: Commence an oxytocin infusion 40 units in 1000 mL Hartmann's at 250 mL/hour, give intramuscular ergometrine 250 micrograms and add intravenous tranexamic acid 1 g over 10 minutes
options.rationale: Ergometrine is contraindicated in chronic hypertension because it can precipitate hypertensive crisis. (Source: RANZCOG: PPH)

explanation.summary: Primary postpartum haemorrhage management follows the four T causes (tone, trauma, tissue, thrombin) with simultaneous resuscitation and uterotonic escalation. Uterine atony is treated with an oxytocin infusion, ergometrine, misoprostol and carboprost in sequence. Ergometrine is contraindicated in hypertension; carboprost is contraindicated in asthma. Tranexamic acid 1 g IV is given within 3 hours of onset as per WOMAN trial evidence. Balloon tamponade or surgical haemostasis follows if pharmacological measures fail.
key_points: Uterotonic ladder: oxytocin, ergometrine, misoprostol, carboprost
Ergometrine contraindicated in hypertension
Carboprost contraindicated in asthma
Tranexamic acid 1 g IV within 3 hours of onset
Balloon tamponade if pharmacological measures fail
explanation.pearls: Use a rule of '1 g, 1 g, 1 g' aide-memoire in PPH: 1 g tranexamic acid IV, 1 g fibrinogen concentrate if hypofibrinogenaemia, and a target packed-cell to FFP ratio of 1:1 in major obstetric haemorrhage activation.
